APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/representation-learning topic—morphology-aware specialization of word vectors—and evaluates with community-standard intrinsic (SimLex, SimVerb) and extrinsic (DST on WOZ) benchmarks across multiple languages, so it sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues. The methodology (post-hoc ATTRACT-REPEL fine-tuning from simple language-specific rule sets), comparison to a wide range of strong baselines, and reporting of hyperparameters align with expected experimental standards; my only caution is the summary’s lack of explicit code release, which affects reproducibility but does not diminish topical fit. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it addresses word representation refinement and semantic specialisation with multilingual and morphologically-aware techniques. The method (post-hoc ATTRACT/REPEL fine-tuning using simple language-specific morphological rules) and evaluations (SimLex, SimVerb, Morph-SimLex and a real downstream DST task with notable German/Italian gains) match community standards and baselines. Positioning is appropriate for the venue — it speaks to lexical semantics, morphology modelling, and practical downstream impact while acknowledging limitations (non-exhaustive rules, variable gains across languages). Overall the work is highly relevant to the conference audience and evaluation norms.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/representation-learning topic—morphology-aware specialization of word vectors—and evaluates with community-standard intrinsic (SimLex, SimVerb) and extrinsic (DST on WOZ) benchmarks across multiple languages, so it sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues. The methodology (post-hoc ATTRACT-REPEL fine-tuning from simple language-specific rule sets), comparison to a wide range of strong baselines, and reporting of hyperparameters align with expected experimental standards; my only caution is the summary’s lack of explicit code release, which affects reproducibility but does not diminish topical fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it addresses word representation refinement and semantic specialisation with multilingual and morphologically-aware techniques. The method (post-hoc ATTRACT/REPEL fine-tuning using simple language-specific morphological rules) and evaluations (SimLex, SimVerb, Morph-SimLex and a real downstream DST task with notable German/Italian gains) match community standards and baselines. Positioning is appropriate for the venue — it speaks to lexical semantics, morphology modelling, and practical downstream impact while acknowledging limitations (non-exhaustive rules, variable gains across languages). Overall the work is highly relevant to the conference audience and evaluation norms.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an important practical problem—improving low-frequency word vector estimates for morphologically rich languages—using a lightweight, language-specific rule-based post-processing that avoids curated lexica. The demonstrated large, consistent intrinsic gains (SimLex, Morph-SimLex) and substantial downstream improvements in Dialogue State Tracking for German and Italian indicate the results would change how practitioners specialise embeddings for morphology-rich languages. The method’s portability and low-resource requirements open clear follow-up work (broader languages, richer rule sets, integration with training) and make it likely to be adopted and built upon. While not revolutionary, it is a strong, widely relevant contribution to representation learning for morphology. | B(4): This paper tackles an important practical gap—improving low-frequency word vectors for morphologically rich languages—using a lightweight, portable post-processing method that avoids curated lexica. The consistent intrinsic improvements (SimLex variants) and substantial downstream gains in DST for German and Italian demonstrate real-world relevance and will likely change how practitioners approach morphological signal in word representations. While the reliance on simple, language-specific rule sets may limit universality (and yields smaller gains for morphologically poor languages like English), the approach opens clear directions for scalable morphological constraint extraction and multilingual specialisation. Overall, the contribution is likely to be widely adopted and extended, though not fully transformative given its rule-based scope.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an important practical problem—improving low-frequency word vector estimates for morphologically rich languages—using a lightweight, language-specific rule-based post-processing that avoids curated lexica. The demonstrated large, consistent intrinsic gains (SimLex, Morph-SimLex) and substantial downstream improvements in Dialogue State Tracking for German and Italian indicate the results would change how practitioners specialise embeddings for morphology-rich languages. The method’s portability and low-resource requirements open clear follow-up work (broader languages, richer rule sets, integration with training) and make it likely to be adopted and built upon. While not revolutionary, it is a strong, widely relevant contribution to representation learning for morphology.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical gap—improving low-frequency word vectors for morphologically rich languages—using a lightweight, portable post-processing method that avoids curated lexica. The consistent intrinsic improvements (SimLex variants) and substantial downstream gains in DST for German and Italian demonstrate real-world relevance and will likely change how practitioners approach morphological signal in word representations. While the reliance on simple, language-specific rule sets may limit universality (and yields smaller gains for morphologically poor languages like English), the approach opens clear directions for scalable morphological constraint extraction and multilingual specialisation. Overall, the contribution is likely to be widely adopted and extended, though not fully transformative given its rule-based scope.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The core optimisation (ATTRACT-REPEL specialisation and regularisation toward initial vectors) is already established in prior work (e.g. semantic specialisation / Attract-Repel methods), so the technical novelty is limited. The paper’s main new element is deriving ATTRACT/REPEL constraints from simple, language-specific morphological rules and demonstrating that this inexpensive rule-based constraint extraction yields consistent intrinsic and downstream gains (notably DST). That is a useful and practical extension with clear differentiation from prior lexicon-based specialisation, but it reads as an incremental, non-trivial application rather than a fundamentally new method or problem formulation; an expert would find the idea informative but not surprising. | B(3): The paper does not introduce a fundamentally new optimisation or theoretical framework—its core Attract–Repel specialisation derives from prior semantic-specialisation work (e.g., the 2017 Attract-Repel line). Its principal novelty lies in the pragmatic idea of harvesting simple language-specific morphological rules as a lightweight source of ATTRACT/REPEL constraints and demonstrating multilingual/downstream gains, which is a useful and moderately novel application but largely an incremental extension rather than a paradigm shift. The authors clearly differentiate their contribution (rule-based constraint extraction, multilingual portability, DST results) from prior lexicon-driven specialisation, so the work is notable but not highly original to an expert in the area.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from motivation through method (Morph-fitting) to experiments and conclusion; the main contributions are stated early and the methodology and datasets are described. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (intrinsic and downstream results) effectively convey improvements across languages. Writing quality is generally good with thorough related-work coverage, though a few methodological choices (e.g., exact rule extraction procedure and code availability) could be more explicitly described to aid reproducibility. Overall clarity is high but there are minor presentation gaps that prevent an exceptional rating. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear intro → method → experiments → conclusion flow and states its main contributions early (morph-fitting approach, portability via simple rule sets, and downstream DST gains). Notation and experimental details appear consistently reported (hyperparameters and datasets are listed), and related work coverage is thorough, which aids reader navigation. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as informative and the writing quality is good, though there are minor presentation issues (e.g., some limitations and the gap between intrinsic and extrinsic gains could be discussed more explicitly) that prevent a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from motivation through method (Morph-fitting) to experiments and conclusion; the main contributions are stated early and the methodology and datasets are described. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (intrinsic and downstream results) effectively convey improvements across languages. reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear intro → method → experiments → conclusion flow and states its main contributions early (morph-fitting approach, portability via simple rule sets, and downstream DST gains). Notation and experimental details appear consistently reported (hyperparameters and datasets are listed), and related work coverage is thorough, which aids reader navigation. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as informative and the writing quality is good, though there are minor presentation issues (e.g., some limitations and the gap between intrinsic and extrinsic gains could be discussed more explicitly) that prevent a top score.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear claims and supports them with multi-language intrinsic evaluations (SimLex, SimVerb, Morph-SimLex) and a downstream DST task where morph-fitting yields large gains and SOTA for German/Italian; it also compares to reasonable baselines and reports hyperparameters. However, the summary does not mention code or dataset release, variance across runs, or statistical significance testing, and the portability claim rests on simple non-exhaustive rule sets without thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses to isolate rule coverage and hyperparameter effects. These are minor but important reproducibility and robustness gaps that do not overturn the experimental findings, hence a score of 4. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are generally supported by multi-language experiments (SimLex-999, SimVerb-3500, Morph-SimLex-999) and a downstream DST evaluation showing gains for German and Italian, and hyperparameters are reported and compared against a wide set of baselines (SGNS, GloVe, Count-SVD, etc.). There are no novel mathematical derivations to validate beyond applying ATTRACT-REPEL, so technical correctness there is plausible. However, the summary does not report statistical significance testing or code release, and the approach relies on language-specific non-exhaustive rule sets—assumptions that create modest unsupported generalisability claims and a noted mismatch between intrinsic and extrinsic gains. Overall the methodology is sound with minor evidentiary gaps that do not overturn the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and supports them with multi-language intrinsic evaluations (SimLex, SimVerb, Morph-SimLex) and a downstream DST task where morph-fitting yields large gains and SOTA for German/Italian; it also compares to reasonable baselines and reports hyperparameters. However, the summary does not mention code or dataset release, variance across runs, or statistical significance testing, and the portability claim rests on simple non-exhaustive rule sets without thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses to isolate rule coverage and hyperparameter effects. These are minor but important reproducibility and robustness gaps that do not overturn the experimental findings, hence a score of 4.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are generally supported by multi-language experiments (SimLex-999, SimVerb-3500, Morph-SimLex-999) and a downstream DST evaluation showing gains for German and Italian, and hyperparameters are reported and compared against a wide set of baselines (SGNS, GloVe, Count-SVD, etc.). There are no novel mathematical derivations to validate beyond applying ATTRACT-REPEL, so technical correctness there is plausible. However, the summary does not report statistical significance testing or code release, and the approach relies on language-specific non-exhaustive rule sets—assumptions that create modest unsupported generalisability claims and a noted mismatch between intrinsic and extrinsic gains. Overall the methodology is sound with minor evidentiary gaps that do not overturn the main conclusions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are broad—multiple intrinsic benchmarks (SimLex, SimVerb, Morph-SimLex) and a substantive downstream task (multilingual WOZ DST) across several languages—with comparisons to reasonable baselines and reporting of hyperparameters, which supports the main claims. However, the paper lacks reported ablations/sensitivity analyses that would isolate the contributions of ATTRACT vs REPEL and of specific rule components, and there is no mention of code or exact preprocessing/tokenisation details needed for full reproducibility. Related work on semantic specialisation (e.g. Attract-Repel style methods) appears acknowledged, and limitations (rule non-exhaustiveness, language differences) are discussed, but statistical robustness (variance across runs/significance) and more thorough error analyses are missing. | B(4): The experimental evaluation is strong: multiple intrinsic benchmarks (SimLex-999, Morph-SimLex, SimVerb-3500) and a meaningful downstream task (multilingual DST) support the core claims, and the paper reports hyperparameters and compares to relevant baselines. The authors include useful negative/failure observations (e.g., limited gains for English, Morph-fixed vectors harming DST) and position the work relative to prior semantic-specialisation methods (e.g., Attract-Repel lineage). Minor gaps remain in sensitivity/ablation depth (e.g., more systematic analysis of rule set coverage, impact of REPEL vs ATTRACT contributions across languages), so while thorough overall the analysis stops short of exhaustive robustness checks and deeper error analysis across low-frequency strata.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are broad—multiple intrinsic benchmarks (SimLex, SimVerb, Morph-SimLex) and a substantive downstream task (multilingual WOZ DST) across several languages—with comparisons to reasonable baselines and reporting of hyperparameters, which supports the main claims. However, the paper lacks reported ablations/sensitivity analyses that would isolate the contributions of ATTRACT vs REPEL and of specific rule components, and there is no mention of code or exact preprocessing/tokenisation details needed for full reproducibility. Related work on semantic specialisation (e.g. Attract-Repel style methods) appears acknowledged, and limitations (rule non-exhaustiveness, language differences) are discussed, but statistical robustness (variance across runs/significance) and more thorough error analyses are missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is strong: multiple intrinsic benchmarks (SimLex-999, Morph-SimLex, SimVerb-3500) and a meaningful downstream task (multilingual DST) support the core claims, and the paper reports hyperparameters and compares to relevant baselines. The authors include useful negative/failure observations (e.g., limited gains for English, Morph-fixed vectors harming DST) and position the work relative to prior semantic-specialisation methods (e.g., Attract-Repel lineage). Minor gaps remain in sensitivity/ablation depth (e.g., more systematic analysis of rule set coverage, impact of REPEL vs ATTRACT contributions across languages), so while thorough overall the analysis stops short of exhaustive robustness checks and deeper error analysis across low-frequency strata.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to a broad set of strong baselines (SGNS variants, GloVe, Count-SVD, SymPat-Emb, Context2Vec, MultiCCA, BiSkip, and task-specific baselines) and evaluates on standard intrinsic and extrinsic metrics, which supports fair head-to-head comparison. It builds directly on the Attract-Repel semantic-specialisation line (cited) and discusses limitations and when gains are small (e.g., English), indicating awareness of prior methods performing comparably in some settings. Minor gaps remain: the summary does not state code/data release for exact reproducibility or whether statistical variance/significance across runs was reported, and I did not see explicit ablation isolating rule-sets versus optimisation choices, so I mark it as strong but not fully comprehensive. | B(5): The paper benchmarks against a wide and appropriate set of baselines (SGNS/GloVe/Count-SVD, SymPat-Emb, MultiCCA, BiSkip, Context2Vec and a morph-fixed ablation) and evaluates on standard intrinsic (SimLex, SimVerb, Morph-SimLex) and extrinsic (WOZ DST) tasks using the same metrics and corpora—supporting fair comparisons. It explicitly builds on and appears to cite the relevant Attract-Repel line of work (semantic specialisation) and discusses when prior approaches are competitive or limited (e.g., morphologically simple languages, morph-fixed failure). The authors also analyze differences between their morph-rule-derived constraints and curated lexica, and report both intrinsic vs extrinsic discrepancies, indicating thorough, balanced comparison and positioning relative to prior art.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to a broad set of strong baselines (SGNS variants, GloVe, Count-SVD, SymPat-Emb, Context2Vec, MultiCCA, BiSkip, and task-specific baselines) and evaluates on standard intrinsic and extrinsic metrics, which supports fair head-to-head comparison. It builds directly on the Attract-Repel semantic-specialisation line (cited) and discusses limitations and when gains are small (e.g., English), indicating awareness of prior methods performing comparably in some settings. Minor gaps remain: the summary does not state code/data release for exact reproducibility or whether statistical variance/significance across runs was reported, and I did not see explicit ablation isolating rule-sets versus optimisation choices, so I mark it as strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper benchmarks against a wide and appropriate set of baselines (SGNS/GloVe/Count-SVD, SymPat-Emb, MultiCCA, BiSkip, Context2Vec and a morph-fixed ablation) and evaluates on standard intrinsic (SimLex, SimVerb, Morph-SimLex) and extrinsic (WOZ DST) tasks using the same metrics and corpora—supporting fair comparisons. It explicitly builds on and appears to cite the relevant Attract-Repel line of work (semantic specialisation) and discusses when prior approaches are competitive or limited (e.g., morphologically simple languages, morph-fixed failure). The authors also analyze differences between their morph-rule-derived constraints and curated lexica, and report both intrinsic vs extrinsic discrepancies, indicating thorough, balanced comparison and positioning relative to prior art.

main_claims: Morph-fitting, a post-processing method using simple language-specific morphological rules, improves low-frequency word vector estimates.
Morph-fitting boosts the overall semantic quality of word vector collections, yielding large consistent improvements on intrinsic similarity benchmarks (e.g., SimLex-999 and related sets).
Morph-fitted vectors produce large gains in a downstream task (Dialogue State Tracking), setting new state-of-the-art scores for German and Italian in their experiments.
The approach obviates curated semantic lexicons by extracting morphological constraints from simple, non-exhaustive rule sets, making it portable to multiple languages.
_summary.methodology: A post-processing semantic specialisation pipeline that derives ATTRACT (pull-similar) and REPEL (push-antonymous) constraints from simple language-specific inflectional and derivational rules and applies the ATTRACT-REPEL optimisation to fine-tune pre-trained distributional word vectors, while regularising towards the original vectors.
limitations: Morph-fitting yields little variation / benefit for morphologically simple languages such as English (English shows little downstream gain).
Morph-fixed vectors (fixing to highest-frequency inflectional form) do not enhance DST performance and can eliminate useful semantic content.
The morphological rule sets used are non-exhaustive (simple rule sets were intentionally used).
There is a noted discrepancy between intrinsic (similarity) gains and corresponding extrinsic (downstream) gains.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - word representation and morphology (semantic specialisation)

reviewer_a.name: Methodical Experimentalist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher with extensive experience in reproducible NLP experiments, benchmark evaluation, and developing and validating representation learning methods for language tasks.
traits: insists on clear baselines, exact experimental protocol descriptions, and availability of code and data to reproduce results
looks for thorough ablation studies and sensitivity analyses (e.g., hyperparameters, rule coverage, corpus size, vector initialisation)
evaluates statistical rigor: significance testing, variance reporting across runs, and robustness to dataset splits
notices implementation details that affect comparability (preprocessing, tokenisation, handling of OOVs, training objectives)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments are designed and reported so claims are supported and reproducible, and requests targeted analyses to isolate the contributions of each component.

reviewer_b.name: Language-Impact Strategist
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher specializing in lexical semantics, morphology, and applied dialogue systems, with a track record of evaluating cross-lingual and low-resource techniques and their real-world impact.
traits: values clear positioning relative to prior work on semantic specialisation, morphological modelling, and lexicon-based constraints
assesses novelty and generalisability: whether simple rule-based approaches meaningfully advance the field or merely repackage existing ideas
checks downstream relevance and real-world impact, especially multilingual and low-frequency word behaviour
looks for discussion of linguistic limitations, language coverage, and practical trade-offs (manual rule crafting vs curated lexica)
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper’s significance and novelty in the broader research landscape and judges whether the proposed approach meaningfully advances practical and scientific goals in lexical semantics and multilingual NLP.
